red.name: Ile de France
red.didYouKnow: A single red tulip bulb once sold for the price of a house in 17th century Amsterdam. People mortgaged their homes just to own one.
red.symbolizes: True love and passion. Giving someone red tulips is a declaration of deep, undying love — stronger than roses in Dutch tradition.
red.history: Red tulips arrived in Europe from the Ottoman Empire in the 1500s. Sultan Suleiman the Magnificent considered them sacred and only royalty could grow them.

yellow.name: Strong Gold
yellow.didYouKnow: Yellow tulips get their colour from carotenoids — the same pigment that makes carrots orange and flamingos pink.
yellow.symbolizes: Cheerfulness, hope and new beginnings. In Victorian times yellow flowers were sent to wish someone good luck on a new adventure.
yellow.history: The word tulip comes from the Turkish word for turban — because the flower shape looked like the headwear worn at the Ottoman court in the 1500s.

purple.name: Negrita
purple.didYouKnow: Purple dye was once so rare and expensive that only kings and emperors could afford it. Purple flowers carried the same status symbol.
purple.symbolizes: Royalty, admiration and respect. Giving purple tulips means you see someone as truly extraordinary — above ordinary praise.
purple.history: Negrita has won more international flower awards than almost any other tulip variety. It was bred specifically to achieve the deepest, richest purple possible.

white.name: Pays Bas
white.didYouKnow: The Ottawa Tulip Festival exists because of white tulips. In 1945 the Dutch royal family sent 100,000 bulbs to thank Canada for sheltering them during WW2.
white.symbolizes: Forgiveness, purity and new beginnings. In the Netherlands white tulips are given as an apology or as a fresh start between two people.
white.history: Princess Juliana of the Netherlands gave birth to Princess Margriet in Ottawa in 1943. The Canadian government temporarily declared the hospital room Dutch territory so the princess would be Dutch-born.

pink.name: Prominence
pink.didYouKnow: Pink tulips are called Peony tulips because their layered double blooms look almost identical to peonies — people often mix them up at markets.
pink.symbolizes: Affection, caring and happiness. Pink tulips say 'I care about you' without the intensity of red — perfect for friends and family.
pink.history: Pink is the most photographed tulip colour at the Ottawa festival every year. Prominence was bred to be the perfect photography subject — its petals reflect light evenly in any condition.

orange.name: Orange Emperor
orange.didYouKnow: Orange is the national colour of the Netherlands — named after the Dutch Royal House of Orange. Orange tulips are planted every year near the Dutch embassy worldwide.
orange.symbolizes: Energy, enthusiasm and warmth. Orange tulips are given to someone you want to cheer up or celebrate — they are the happiest colour in the tulip world.
orange.history: Orange Emperor is one of the first tulips to bloom each spring, often pushing through snow. Dutch growers say if Orange Emperor blooms, winter is officially over.
